data.title: Sell AGCO Stock, Says Morgan Stanley. Wall Street Sure Can Be Confusing.
data.description:
Al Root

AGCO stock fell Tuesday after catching a downgrade. The rating action came right after another brokerage upgraded shares.

Wall Street sure can be confusing. There's a lesson for investors in that idea observation.

Tuesday, Morgan Stanley analyst Angel Castillo downgraded AGCO stock to Sell from Hold. His price target went to $75 from $94 a share.

Shares of the agricultural equipment maker were down 4.99% at $80.06in midday trading, while the S&P 500 and Dow Jones Industrial Average were down 2.25% and 1.42%, respectively.

"While we continue to believe that the Ag equipment cycle is troughing, we see downside risks to earnings," wrote Castillo, adding that dealer inventories are relatively high — AGCO sells to dealers and dealers then sell to farmers — and that there are business-execution risks from combining Trimble's precision ag business with AGCO's.

Trimble and AGCO closed a joint venture in 2024 to create a leader in precision ag technology that helps farmers use fewer inputs, such as fertilizer and pesticides, without sacrificing total output.

There have also been slower than expected market-share gains for AGCO's Fendt brand, he says.

It sounds ominous. Still, the downgrade came about a week after Citi analyst Kyle Menges upgraded shares to Buy from Hold.

There are headwinds, such as those Castillo mentioned, but the farming cycle is near a bottom. Farm income in 2024 amounted to about $150 billion, according to the U.S. Department of Agriculture, down from a peak of some $225 billion in 2022. Farm income becomes cash for farmers to spend on products from companies such as AGCO. It earned $15.55 a share in 2023. Wall Street projects $3.94 in 2025.

So, where does that leave investors?

As always, any one upgrade or downgrade goes into the Wall Street consensus. Overall, 35% of analysts covering AGCO stock rate shares Buy. The average Buy-rating ratio for stocks in the S&P 500 is about 55%. About 12% of analysts rate AGCO shares Sell. The average Sell-rating ratio for stocks in the S&P 500 is about 7%.

It's fair to say the Street is lukewarm on AGCO stock now and that analysts are waiting to see a turn in commodity prices and farm income before getting more positive.

That opinion is reflected in Deere ratings, too. Only 42% of analysts covering Deere stock call shares a Buy.

Sentiment hasn't turned yet. A year ago, 50% of analysts covering AGCO stock rated shares Buy, and 52% rated Deere shares Buy.

Investors can pay attention to each comment. But they shouldn't overreact, and they should form their own opinions about stocks and industry outlook — with the help of Wall Street, of course.

Write to Al Root at allen.root@dowjones.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Azure Printed Homes Achieves 3 Year Milestones, Completing 3rd Round of Funding on April 30, 2025
data.description:
LOS ANGELES, April 16, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) — Azure Printed Homes, a pioneer in sustainable 3D-printed modular and prefab housing, is completing three years of business and its third round of funding on April 30, 2025. The company was founded in March 2022 as an outgrowth of Azure Construction in California and has raised $6 million so far (including $1.3 million in venture capital) and has $35 million in pre-orders. The company’s current crowdfund campaign will end on 4/30/25, with the share price discount ending on 4/26/25 (full information on the investment is available here.)

The company’s primary factory location, which is currently expanding, is based in Los Angeles, with a Denver, Colorado factory slated to open during 2025.

Says Azure Cofounder Gene Eidelman, “We are a U.S. manufacturer that is immune to tariffs and are scaling as fast as possible to address the current needs. Additionally, we are the only modular manufacturer based in L.A., which is making us an ideal resource to assist in rebuilding at scale following the recent LA Fires.”

The company has completed more than 100 homes so far using technology that is 70% faster and 30% less expensive than traditional construction, Eidelman said.

In addition to primary homes, ADUs, studios and tiny homes, the company is increasingly prevalent in developments of transitional housing to address the growing problem of homelessness. Outdoor hospitality and Glamping resorts are other common buyers of Azure homes. Azure is the manufacturer of choice of units for glamping resorts in multiple states including California, Georgia, Arkansas, Tennessee and Texas.

By 2030, the company intends to scale to be manufacturing 2500 homes/year.

About Azure Printed Homes

Azure Printed Homes revolutionizes the construction industry with 3D printing technology that transforms recycled materials into high-quality, energy-efficient housing. Built 70% faster and 30% more cost-effectively than traditional methods, Azure’s product line includes primary homes, tiny homes, ADUs, and transitional housing designed to tackle housing shortages worldwide. For more information on investment visit https://invest.azureprintedhomes.com.

For media inquiries, please contact:Cheryl Conner, SnappConner PR for Azure

info@snappconner.com

801-806-0150

A photo accompanying this announcement is available at https://www.globenewswire.com/NewsRoom/AttachmentNg/d95a2fb9-99e0-4337-a6a0-31ab97b6cd3c

SXSW 2025 Innovation Award, Urban Experience

Azure Printed Homes CEO and Co-Founder Ross Maguire Accepting the SXSW Innovation Award for Urban Experience, March 10, 2025

data.title: US mulls penalties to block DeepSeek from buying American technology, NYT reports
data.description: The Trump administration is weighing penalties that would block China's DeepSeek from buying U.S. technology and debating barring Americans' access to its services, the New York Times reported on Wednesday.

data.title: Puerto Rico goes dark after widespread power plant failure
data.description:
Puerto Rico was hit with a massive power outage on Wednesday after energy plants across the island unexpectedly shut down, power generator Genera said.

Puerto Rico has long dealt with chronic power outages, including a blackout on New Year's Eve, as its infrastructure crumbles.

Genera added in a post on X it was working to bring backup units online and investigate the cause of the incident.

Energy distribution company LUMA Energy said the outage began around 12:40 p.m. local time. As of 2 p.m., all plants on the U.S. territory were still offline, real-time data from Genera's website showed.

"We will provide updates as soon as more details become available regarding the cause of the outage and the timeframe for restoring service," Governor Jenniffer Gonzalez said on X.

data.title: Republican town halls bring arrests, stun guns and voter worries about Trump
data.description:
Key points:
  • Republicans face criticism over Trump's policies at town halls
  • Protesters arrested at Greene's town hall in Georgia
  • Grassley confronted over tariffs and social program cuts

The few congressional Republicans still willing to hold in-person town hall meetings in their home states are facing heated criticism this week over President Donald Trump's policies and the prospect of deep cuts to social safety programs including Medicaid.

With the Republican-controlled Congress preparing to move forward on Trump's agenda to cut taxes, tighten border security, promote fossil fuels and bolster military spending, the feedback lawmakers receive from constituents could determine how far they go in cutting environmental initiatives, the Medicaid healthcare program and others that benefit low-income Americans.

"If the town halls are filled with angry constituents worried about the impact of budget cuts, that's going to bring some cold feedback to Washington. If the reaction in the district is more muted, members of Congress might come back and say 'full speed ahead'," said Jessica Riedl, a former Senate aide who is now a senior fellow at the right-leaning Manhattan Institute.

An overflow crowd in Fort Madison, Iowa, confronted Senator Chuck Grassley with concerns about the impact of Trump's tariffs on farmers, the potential effect of spending cuts on low-income Americans to pay for tax cuts and what some viewed as a need for Congress to check Trump's disregard for federal courts.

Congressional Republicans are debating how sharply to cut spending to cover the costs of extending Trump's 2017 tax cuts, which nonpartisan analysts could add more than $5 trillion to the nation's $36.6 trillion in debt.

Three protesters were arrested, including two who were overpowered by police with stun guns, at a town hall meeting in Georgia hosted by Representative Marjorie Taylor Greene, who dismissed concerns about tariffs and spending cuts as a result of brainwashing by the news media.

Grassley and Greene are among a relatively few Republican lawmakers to hold in-person town halls during their ongoing two-week spring recess, after several public meetings erupted into shouting matches earlier this year over the Trump agenda and efforts by billionaire Elon Musk's Department of Government Efficiency to fire federal workers and cut federal programs.

Reuters/Ipsos polling this month shows that 43% of Americans approve of Trump's job performance so far, with the president getting poor marks on his handling of the economy and inflation — key campaign issues — and respondents wary of his campaign to remake the federal government.

Trump's approval rating remained higher than it was for most of his first term in office, and above what Democratic President Joe Biden enjoyed for most of his term.

"Why won't you do your job, senator?" Grassley was asked by a man who complained that Trump's deportation operation had sent people to prison overseas without due process.

Others alleged the Trump administration was defying court orders in refusing to work to return Kilmar Abrego Garcia from an El Salvador jail after admitting that the Maryland resident, who was living in the U.S. illegally, had been deported in error.

JEERS AND CHANTS

Jeers ensued when Grassley, who chairs the Senate Judiciary Committee, said Congress has no power to return Abrego Garcia and that El Salvador is not subject to the U.S. Supreme Court.

Faced with questions about potential cuts to social safety-net programs, the 91-year-old Republican asked one audience member if she favored work requirements for a federal nutrition program that aids low-income families.

"No. That sounds like waste, fraud and abuse," the woman replied, while another audience member suggested that administering work requirements could increase the cost of the program.

A similar wave of constituent anger erupted in former President Barack Obama's first term when conservative Tea Party activists disrupted public events, a signal of anger ahead of the 2010 midterm elections when the Democrats lost control of the House of Representatives.

In Acworth, Georgia, Greene took no direct questions from the audience. Instead, she read off pre-screened queries, including some that expressed concerns about possible adverse impacts from Trump's tariffs and potential government funding cuts to Medicaid and Social Security.

"Well, Sara, unfortunately, you're being brainwashed by the news that you're watching," the firebrand conservative replied to one questioner. "As far as Medicaid, Social Security, libraries and more, what we're doing is, we're removing waste, fraud and abuse."

Greene, a leading advocate for people who were arrested during the January 6, 2021, attack on the U.S. Capitol by Trump supporters, told her audience at the outset of the meeting that boisterous objections would not be tolerated.

"This is a town hall. This is not a political rally. This is not a protest. If you stand up and want to protest, if you want to shout and chant, we will have you removed," Greene said.

Acworth police said six people were removed from the meeting, including a man who shouted "False!" when the conservative firebrand called Democrats "the party of violence."

One protester was shot with a stun gun at the back of the room after scuffling with police, as some audience members cheered and applauded.

"This is a peaceful town hall," Greene intoned as several police officers hustled him out the door.

Attendants were required to register for the event ahead of time and provide identification showing that they lived within Greene's district.

Greene's town hall ended soon after police escorted out a man shaking his fist in the air. "That's an illegal alien!" the congresswoman exclaimed. Members of the audience chanted: "USA! USA! USA!"

data.title: Tariffs Are Roiling Markets. Here's What Financial Advisors Are Telling Clients.
data.description:
By Steve Garmhausen

The haphazard rollout of President Donald Trump's stiff tariffs has rattled the stock and bond markets and raised fears of recession and inflation. The current uncertainty marks a striking shift from the broad optimism at the beginning of the year and has created pressure on financial advisors to explain to clients what's going on and how they should react. For this week's Barron's Advisor Big Q, we asked four financial professionals to weigh in.

Jim Carroll, senior wealth advisor and portfolio manager, Ballast Rock Private Wealth: This may prove to be one of the most volatile periods in market history. Some of the things that we've seen happen over the past two weeks haven't happened in the history of the markets. We saw the largest day-over-day drop in VIX history in one day. We saw four days where the VIX [ the Cboe volatility index] moved more than 20 points intraday. We've seen 10 three-standard-deviation changes in VIX futures since last August. There's little reason to believe we're on the other side of this, given uncertainties that I don't think are going away anytime soon. Let's assume for the moment that there is a plan to restore American manufacturing and reduce our reliance on potentially unreliable trade partners. Getting from here to there at the speed that's being proposed is going to be rough. It's going to be volatile.

We're reminding clients that, to paraphrase Mike Tyson, everybody has tremendous risk tolerance until they get punched in the face. We've been punched in the face. It's a reminder that markets can be very volatile, that markets don't just go up in a straight line year after year, that being overly concentrated in seven stocks was never really a good idea and probably is less of a good idea today. I think this is a time where we really almost need to start fresh with every client in a planning process and make sure that whatever portfolio we construct is one that we and they feel gives them a chance to get to the other side of this mess. At that point if it makes sense to increase the expected returns and risk of the portfolio, great. But we don't think this is a time to "buy the dip." Because we think there's a decent chance that the dip could be deeper.

Amy Hamasaki, owner, president, Mountain Wealth Planning: Most economists will agree that tariffs are inflationary and as prices increase, this will slow consumer spending, consumption, and that will eventually lead to lower GDP growth that would pave the way for possible stagflation. The way these tariffs have been announced has created a lot of uncertainty. Changes are happening minute by minute, and companies are making huge decisions about where manufacturing will go. That creates a lot of uncertainty. The three primary industries all of this is really impacting right now are pharmaceuticals, apparel, and automotive. To create a new pharmaceutical plant, for example, takes a really long time. So leaders of these huge mega corporations are left scratching their heads. That in itself will lead to lower growth.

I'm telling clients to have a little tilt to the international side, both developed and emerging, to help buffer internal U.S. markets. Diversification is key, along with maintaining ample cash reserves of between three and 12 months of basic living expenses. Because if we have slower economic growth, we're probably going to have higher levels of unemployment. And focus on the long haul, because looking at these short-term market movements can be very disconcerting. Stay true to your objectives and don't react emotionally to any of the whipsaw movements right now. A lot of people want to sell, and others want to buy at these lower prices. I say let's maintain a stable strategic investment strategy, with no changes at this time. Stay the course.

Anthony Saglimbene, chief market strategist, Ameriprise Financial: We're telling our clients that we've been here before. When you look at the stock market going back to 1950, we've had lots of event shocks. We've had many recessions. We've been here in terms of uncertainty and volatility. If you're investing based on your goals and following a well-diversified strategy, we generally see those types of portfolios carry through the volatility. Stock prices ultimately recover, and that diversified portfolio helps insulate some of that risk. It keeps you invested so you don't make what could be very poor decisions during times of stress.

If the tariff rates we have right now stay in effect, I believe global growth is likely to slow, corporate profitability to come down, and the odds of a recession to go up. Over the near term, growth conditions could look worse than what most investors figured would be the case at the start of the year. However, if we see better bilateral trade agreements, if we see tariff rates come down, particularly for Canada, Mexico, and Europe, then I think the anxiety and fear may be less warranted. I think stocks have priced in a recession and some of the worst-case scenarios from the tariffs. So I think additional downside is limited. And if we do see negotiations and less-steep tariffs on close trading partners, you could see stocks recover a little. But I don't think this volatility is going to change much until we have more clarity about what these tariffs mean for the economy and corporate profits.

Alex Rykken, f inancial advisor, RBC Wealth Management : This feels like March 2020. It was early Covid, and everybody knew the world was changing in some significant way, but nobody knew how. How tariffs will change the world is new. But this idea of scary things happening in the world is not. In this case, the stock market can't figure out how tariffs impact businesses because we don't know the rules of the game. We're in fear mode right now. Eventually, the world will keep running. Once that happens, we can return to some level of normalcy. In 2020 the markets fell sharply between February and April. By May, we started seeing signs of life again. By the end of the year the market was up about 20%. There is no guarantee the market will react the same way this time around, but we like to remind clients of the circumstances back then.

I remain a believer in American resilience and in capitalism. Owning high-quality companies has for generations been the best way to compound wealth, and I don't think that's changing. Businesses are good at figuring out how to make money. They just need to know the rules of the game. And no one knows the rules of the game right now, but businesses will figure out how to keep doing their thing. The best thing you can do in an extreme environment is to stick to the plan you made in a rational environment. It's too early to know which way this is going to go. But we want to focus on our clients having enough money to live through uncertainty.

Chad Rushing, financial advisor, Freedom Street Partners (Steward Partners): We highlight the importance of active management during times like these. All of our portfolios are research driven and actively managed. What's making things tough during this trade war is the uncertainty. Are we going to have inflation, stagflation, deflation? Who's going to be impacted by the tariffs? Are we going to have long-term tariffs? Those things will resolve themselves over the next few months. As they resolve, you want to make sure you have active managers who can adjust your portfolio to take advantage of the opportunities that are coming.

We also touch on the value of staying invested. People who have parked money in cash are like: Well, at least I'm not losing anything. But they're losing sight of the destruction of buying power. I saw a report the other day that what bought you $10,000 of goods in 1998 buys about $5,100 of goods today. So if you don't have that $10,000 appreciating over the next 25 years, you lose buying power. You safely go broke.

Write to advisor.editors@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Einhorn's Greenlight gained 8.2% in Q1, remains bullish on gold
data.description:
Hedge fund Greenlight Capital gained 8.2% in the first three months of 2025, buoyed by a bet on gold that it said should pay off further as it expects Trump administration policies to continue roiling financial markets.

Greenlight founder David Einhorn told investors in a letter seen by Reuters that the firm handily outpaced the broader Standard & Poor's 500 index thanks mainly to investments in gold, which was the portfolio's biggest winner.

"In late February we pivoted from conservative, but not bearish, to bearish," the letter said, adding: "We suspect we are now in a bear market that is just starting."

The firm owns gold bars and call options, the letter said, adding that gold advanced 19% during the quarter. The S&P 500 slipped 4.3% during the first three months of the year.

Investors seeking hints of market trends have long kept close watch on Einhorn and Greenlight's moves. Research group HFR said the average hedge fund was roughly flat in the first quarter, losing 0.38%, on average.

Greenlight described the Trump administration's approach as "go fast and break things" with policies, including deep job cuts at government agencies and tariffs, that are likely to weigh on consumer sentiment and growth.

During the quarter, Greenlight said it added "several short positions in consumer companies" without identifying them. Greenlight expects their share prices to fall as consumers tighten their belts.

Gold is often a hedge against inflation, and Greenlight said White House policies are likely to stoke inflation. "All current Administration policy roads lead to higher longer-term inflation," the letter said.

Slower growth, Greenlight reasoned, will likely force the U.S. Federal Reserve to cut interest rates more than markets expect and the fund has established a position in SOFR (secured overnight financing rate) futures. It also added tail protection in case the dollar depreciates substantially against the euro and yen.

data.title: Plaza Retail REIT Declares April 2025 Distribution
data.description:
Plaza Retail REIT (PLZ-UN.TO) Wednesday declared its Aprilmonthly distribution in the amount of $0.02333 per unit ($0.28 annualized), consistent with the March distribution.

The April distribution will be payable on May 15 to unitholders of record on April 30.

data.title: SEC Extends Effective and Compliance Dates for Amendments to Investment Company Reporting Requirements
data.description:
Washington, D.C.--(Newsfile Corp. - April 16, 2025) - The Securities and Exchange Commission today announced a two-year extension of the effective and compliance dates for rule amendments adopted in August 2024 that require many types of registered funds to more frequently report portfolio-related information to the Commission and the public on Form N-PORT.

The compliance date for larger fund groups is extended from Nov. 17, 2025, to Nov. 17, 2027, and the compliance date for smaller fund groups is extended from May 18, 2026, to May 18, 2028.

The extension is designed to provide time for the Commission to complete its review of the amendments in accordance with a Presidential Memorandum and take any further appropriate actions, which may include proposed amendments to Form N-PORT.

data.title: Powell Says Market Is 'Struggling' With Uncertainty, Volatility
data.description:
By Aaron Lucchetti

Asked today whether there was a "Fed put" on markets--the idea that the central-bank would cut interest rates if stocks continued their recent decline--Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell offered an explanation that markets' recent selloff was understandable given the high amounts of uncertainty about trade policy.

Markets are "doing what they're supposed to do," he said, in reacting to major changes in policy on a range of issues from trade to immigration. On tariffs, he added the current tariffs under consideration are larger than the Smoot-Hawley tariffs of the 1930s. These are "fundamental changes," he said.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: Holiday-Shortened Week Means More Farmer PlantingMarket Talk
data.description: With the upcoming Good Friday holiday, many farmers are attempting to get their planting work done before the long weekend. "Easter week is typically a quiet and choppy shortened week of trade," says Brian Pullam of Linn & Associates. Pullam adds that grain traders are prepping for weather in the mid-term that may prevent a lot of timely planting from taking place, which has some farmers trying to rush to beat unsupportive weather conditions. Most-active CBOT corn is up 0.3% in afternoon trading, while soybeans rise 0.1% and wheat climbs 0.9%.(kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

data.title: Cloudflare Shares Rise After Upgrade From Mizuho Securities
data.description:
Cloudflare shares rose as much as 4.1% in intraday trading on Wednesday after Mizuho Securities upgraded the stock to outperform from neutral and adjusted the price target to $135 from $140.

Trading volume stood at over 2.3 million shares against a daily average of about 4.3 million.

data.title: Nvidia Chip Restriction Much More Severe than BeforeMarket Talk
data.description: New restrictions on selling semiconductors to China could significantly reduce the market opportunity for American chip companies, DA Davidson analyst Gil Luria tells WSJ. "While the extent of the restrictions will only be clear once the U.S. publishes the details, Nvidia's disclosure indicates the restrictions are much more severe than they were before," Luria says. Nvidia slides 9%, and is the worst performer in the S&P 500. (sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

data.title: Market Talk Roundup: Latest on U.S. Politics
data.description:
Market Talks covering the impact of U.S. Politics and White House policies on companies and markets. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1409 ET - New restrictions on selling semiconductors to China could significantly reduce the market opportunity for American chip companies, DA Davidson analyst Gil Luria tells WSJ. "While the extent of the restrictions will only be clear once the U.S. publishes the details, Nvidia's disclosure indicates the restrictions are much more severe than they were before," Luria says. Nvidia slides 9%, and is the worst performer in the S&P 500. (sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

1353 ET - If the goal of U.S. government restrictions on Nvidia's sales of H20 chips in China was to restrict Beijing's AI capabilities, that ship has already sailed, BofA Securities analyst Vivek Arya tells WSJ. "I understand the goal is to restrict AI, but limiting this product is no way limiting China's AI capability because Huawei Technologies can make products that have similar capabilities," Arya says. The risk is that if Huawei takes its products and is able to sell them into other emerging markets and other places outside of China at a very low price, there is nothing to prevent them from doing that. And it wouldn't help the trade gap either, he adds. "It's hard to defend these moves." Nvidia slides 8%. (sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

1339 ET - The strength in March U.S. retail sales isn't only about consumers trying to get ahead of tariffs, Wells Fargo's Tim Quinlan and Shannon Grein write. Sales beat WSJ consensus in both the headline and the ex-auto figures. The report included upward revisions of previous data. Autos saw the biggest March gain, likely reflecting a rush to buy vehicles before any price increase due to tariffs. But consumers "also spent more at restaurants in March, and we're not aware of a way you can front-run tariffs with a nice night out," Quinlan and Grein say. "How consumers act in these next couple of months will be telling," they say. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1320 ET - Prologis customers are accelerating shipments, rerouting volume and have urgent demand for overflow space as they try to manage tariff volatility, says Tim Arndt, CFO of the biggest industrial REIT, in an analyst call. Free trade zones and bonded warehouses also are being evaluated. Prologis sees inventory levels rising as businesses stockpile and build resiliency, and global markets in its portfolio such as Canada, India and Brazil will become more important. Mexico stands out in terms of growing interest before and after April's tariff announcement, he says. (mark.long@wsj.com)

1310 ET - The government of Canada's most populous province is moving to drop barriers to trade and labor mobility in the country with the introduction of a "Protect Ontario Through Free Trade Within Canada Act." Premier Doug Ford says the economy has long been held back by red tape and regulations, making Canada vulnerable to external threats, including from President Trump's tariffs. The move is part of a wave of support federally and provincially for easing trade within Canada to help counter the hit from Trump's trade policies. Ontario's act wold allow workers from other parts of the country to more easily work in the province, allow more regulated health professions to begin practicing in Ontario while waiting for registration, allow direct-to-consumer alcohol sales with reciprocating provinces, and remove all party-specific exceptions under the Canadian Free Trade Agreement. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

1306 ET - Fitch Ratings says it lowered its forecasts for 2025 world growth by 0.4 percentage points, given the escalation in the global trade war. "World growth is projected to fall below 2% this year, which would be the weakest since 2009 excluding the pandemic," according to Fitch. Growth in the U.S. and in China has been cut by 0.5 pp, according to Fitch. Annual growth in the U.S. is expected at 1.2%, but will slow through 2025 to register just 0.4% year-over-year in 4Q25, Fitch says. In China, growth is expected to fall below 4% this year and in 2026. In the euro zone, growth expectations are stuck below 1%, Fitch adds. (stephen.nakrosis@wsj.com)

1211 ET - As with the March decision, Bank of Canada senior officials debated whether to cut rates again, or hold their fire while awaiting more information. In the end, there was "clear consensus" on keeping the policy rate unchanged after a "really good deliberations" among top policymakers, says BOC's No. 2 official, Carolyn Rogers. She tells reporters there were differing opinions among the seven-member governing council on how big a hit there would be to the economy from US tariffs. Some officials, she says, were "more optimistic that the effects won't be really big." She adds some members suggested "you won't see that really big upward pressure on inflation." More details on the deliberations will emerge in two weeks when the BOC releases minutes of the governing council's discussions. (Paul.Vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

1149 ET--The timeline shared by Nvidia Tuesday afternoon regarding Trump's imposed China-related restrictions for its H20 chips explains the rushed announcement that the graphic chip maker would build $500 billion of AI infrastructure over the next four years, DA Davidson analysts say in a research note. The appeal to the Trump administration was aimed at mitigating the final regulation, the analysts say. "Nvidia may now choose not to follow through on this pledge given the outcome, and if it does, we believe it will erode long-term margins," the analysts add. Nvidia shares fall 6.4% to $105.03. (sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

1129 ET--Nvidia's $5.5 billion charge related to Trump's export restrictions to its H20 chips won't only have a significant impact on the company's first-quarter gross margins, but also its revenue growth during the entire fiscal year, William Blair analysts say in a research note. "While we still believe that Nvidia is the leader in AI infrastructure, with a significant technology moat and robust demand for its next-generation BlackWell GPUs, this will only add to the wall of worry that has built up around the name over the last few months," the analysts say. Shares may be range-bound in the near term as macro uncertainty persists, the analysts say. However, they urge investors "not to miss the forest for the trees" when it comes to a secular winner of the AI platform shift. Shares fall 6.8% to $104.61. (sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

1114 ET - U.S. and eurozone credit assets are still attractive, given the healthy state of corporate balance sheets, manageable debt levels, and availability of cash chasing attractive yields, Societe Generale's Juan Valencia says in a note. "We expect the different credit indices to extend the recent tightening trend," he says. There is a risk, however, that trade tensions drive economies into recession which could hurt credit markets, Societe Generale says. "The risk of a recession in the EU is small (higher and rising in the U.S.), and unless it is a long or deep recession, we believe that spreads will not react as badly as in previous crises." (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

1111 ET - While the Bank of Canada held interest rates steady there is scope for officials to be debating whether they need to lower the policy rate a half percentage point at the next scheduled meeting in June should trade tensions with the U.S. continue to de-escalate, Desjardins' Royce Mendes suggests. He says that doesn't open up the possibility of inter-meeting cuts, just that trade wars tend to be slower moving assuming financial markets remain orderly. Mendes continues to forecast the policy rate will be cut to a low of 1.75% later this year. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

1109 ET - Uncertainty about the path for U.S. trade policy and tariffs and how that fall out for Canada's economy and inflation there has kept the Bank of Canada on the sidelines. Oxford Economics reckons that while a couple more quarter percentage point cuts to the policy rate from the current 2.75% can't be entirely ruled out, it doesn't expect the central bank will lower below 2.25%--the low end of its neutral range--at least until it gets more clarity on the path for trade and fiscal policy. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

data.title: Sector Update: Financial Stocks Decline in Afternoon Trading
data.description:
Financial stocks were lower in Wednesday afternoon trading, with the NYSE Financial Index down 1.1% and the Financial Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLF) dropping 1.7%.

The Philadelphia Housing Index was falling 1.6%, while the Real Estate Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLRE) was up 0.4%.

Bitcoin (BTC/USD) was down 1% to $83,639, and the yield for 10-year US Treasuries was 4.2 basis points lower at 4.28%.

In economic news, the U.S. Census Bureau reported retail sales rose 1.4% in March from the prior month, compared with a 0.2% gain in February and ahead of the FactSet consensus estimate for an increase of 1.3%.

Global trade volume is expected to decline this year as new tariffs fuel uncertainty, with "severe" downside risks that could deal a bigger blow to cross-border imports and exports, the World Trade Organization said Wednesday. Trade volume across the world is expected to decrease 0.2% in 2025 followed by growth of 2.5% in 2026, according to the WTO's latest global trade outlook and statistics report. Trade increased 2.9% in 2024.

In corporate news, KKR is mulling a potential sale of Atlantic Aviation that may value the private jet fixed base operator at $10 billion, Bloomberg reported. KKR shares were down 3%.

US Bancorp reported better-than-expected Q1 results on Wednesday, while the lender reiterated its full-year revenue growth outlook. Its shares still fell 1.8%.

T. Rowe Price Investment Management plans to support activist Starboard Value's director nominees for the board of Autodesk (ADSK), Reuters reported. T. Rowe shares were down 2.5%.

data.title: Farmers delay sales while waiting for rain
data.description:
Spot basis bids for hard red winter (HRW) wheat remained mostly steady in the southern U.S. Plains on Wednesday as farmer selling was minimal, dealers said.

* Farmers are holding off on booking advance sales of the upcoming harvest because of uncertainty over whether rains will arrive to ease harmful dryness, dealers said.

* Warm, dry conditions were further reducing soil moisture in the Plains on Wednesday, according to a daily U.S. Department of Agriculture weather report. Rains are expected this weekend in the region.

* K.C. May hard red winter wheat futures (KWK25) were up 6 cents at $5.59 a bushel by 12:55 p.m. CDT (1755 GMT).

data.title: Luminar Forms Advisory Council to Support Growth Strategy
data.description:
Luminar Technologies said Wednesday that it established an advisory council, Luminar Automotive Council, to help with its growth strategy.

For the council, the company said it has tapped former executives of various automakers including Mercedes-Benz, Tesla , Jaguar Land Rover and Ford Motor , as well as the US Department of Transportation.

The automotive technology company said it is focused on enabling adoption at scale of its light detection and ranging technology, Luminar Halo. The council will help in establishing relationships with potential customers, original equipment manufacturers, contract manufacturing partners and other ecosystem players in facilitating this adoption, Luminar said.

The council members will also advise the company on major industry trends, overall market conditions and the regulatory environment, the company said.

data.title: Volkswagen to keep vehicle prices steady through May
data.description:
Volkswagen confirmed Wednesday that it is joining the growing group of automakers committing to keeping prices steady to ease car shoppers' concerns around tariffs.

Automakers have provided stability, and even deep discounts, to consumers amid worries that tariffs will drive up prices on dealer lots. In a flurry of announcements earlier this month, U.S. and foreign-owned carmakers offered a variety of deals or price assurances, looking to capitalize on a surge of nervous shoppers hoping to clinch a deal.

German carmaker Volkswagen confirmed it is holding prices steady through May, a similar move to Hyundai Motor , which committed to maintaining sticker prices on its current models through June 2.

Ford Motor and Stellantis earlier this month offered shoppers deep discounts across their lineups, and Nissan reduced prices on its 2025 Rogue and 2025 Pathfinder.

While the short-term buying frenzy is a boon to carmakers and their dealers, auto executives have warned that sustained tariffs will add excessive costs to the industry, and ultimately weigh on consumers.

data.title: FED'S POWELL Q&A/ECC: 'ABSOLUTELY' READY TO SUPPLY SWAP LINE DOLLAR FUNDING FOR OTHER CENTRAL BANKS IF NEEDED
Are Foreign Investors Selling U.S. Assets? A Treasury Department Report Will Offer Clues.
data.description:
Karishma Vanjani

Every month, the Treasury Department publishes past data on U.S. assets sold and bought by foreign central banks and investors. No ones typically sweats the numbers — but as President Donald Trump's trade policies continue to roil markets, investors must pay attention to the latest report.

The Treasury is set to release its Treasury International Capital data for February at 4 p.m. Eastern on Wednesday, an official confirmed with Barron's.

The report comes as fears about foreigners punishing Donald Trump's trade policies by selling U.S. assets has gripped financial markets. Stocks saw big swings last week, and the yield on the 10-year U.S. government bonds surged to 4.492% on Friday — its highest level since mid-February.

Higher yields mean someone, somewhere, is selling bonds.

The Treasury data can offer some clarity on foreign selling. Investors should pay special attention to the monthly data on transactions of long-term securities, which have a maturity that's more than one year old. This figure will show whether foreigners have been net sellers or buyers of U.S. Treasuries, corporate bonds, stocks, and other assets.

The report only shows market data from six weeks ago. However, given Trump started announcing global tariffs in February, the report could signal if foreigner central bankers and investors had a knee-jerk reaction to tariffs.

Treasury data so far show investors sold longer-term Treasuries for the three prior consecutive months. In January, foreigners sold a net $13.3 billion of U.S. notes and bonds, $49.69 billion in December, and $34.41 billion in November amid the U.S. election cycle.

Japan is the largest foreign holder of Treasuries; its holdings are down about $250 billion since late 2021. China, meanwhile, is the second-largest holder — and its holdings are at their lowest level since 2009.

Despite geopolitical pressures and past data, no one can say definitively that foreign nations are using their U.S. debt holdings as weapons of financial warfare. China's main state institutions own at least $3 trillion in dollar assets — and deliberately causing a selloff would be self-harming, wrote Mark Williams, Chief Asia Economist at Capital Economics on Monday.

Not only is it in China and other foreign countries' best interest to maintain the value of their U.S. holdings, they also have fewer options. The U.S. Treasury market is by far the safest and the largest in the world. Some of the U.S. companies selling stocks have market capitalizations that are more than a trillion dollars — surpassing the gross domestic product of many emerging nations.

"The only reason a fire sale could make financial sense would be if Chinese officials thought that the US government was about to freeze its assets or default on Chinese-owned bonds," Williams added.

It would be overkill to predict the U.S. will default on its debt, a catastrophic situation that's never occurred — and would almost certainly lead to a recession. However, the idea that the U.S. could freeze assets of foreign nations has heightened after Russia's U.S. funds were frozen in 2022, following the invasion of Ukraine.

Write to Karishma Vanjani at karishma.vanjani@dowjones.com.

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: April Homebuilder Confidence Surprisingly Edges Up
data.description:
US homebuilder confidence unexpectedly picked up in April, though sentiment remained subdued amid heightened economic uncertainty, according to National Association of Home Builders and Wells Fargo data released Wednesday.

The housing market index, which covers new single-family homes, edged higher by one point sequentially to 40 this month. The consensus was for the index to drop to 38, according to a survey compiled by Bloomberg. The March index level of 39 was the lowest in seven months.

"The recent dip in mortgage rates may have pushed some buyers off the fence in March, helping builders with sales activity," NAHB Chairman Buddy Hughes said. "At the same time, builders have expressed growing uncertainty over market conditions as tariffs have increased price volatility for building materials at a time when the industry continues to grapple with labor shortages and a lack of buildable lots."

After announcing sweeping new tariffs on US imports earlier this month, President Donald Trump declared a 90-day pause on certain tariffs for non-retaliating countries. China responded with its own retaliatory duties, and Washington and Beijing have since raised tariffs on each other's goods multiple times.

"Policy uncertainty is having a negative impact on home builders, making it difficult for them to accurately price homes and make critical business decisions," said NAHB Chief Economist Robert Dietz. "The April HMI data indicates that the tariff cost effect is already taking hold, with the majority of builders reporting cost increases on building materials due to tariffs."

Homebuilders' suppliers have increased prices by an average 6.3% due to tariffs, the NAHB survey showed.

The index gauging current sales conditions rose to 45 this month from 43 in March, which was the lowest since December 2023. The metric charting expectations over the next six months dropped four points to 43, the NAHB survey showed.
